items.title: Decision trees as weak learners
items.description: Each boosting round fits a shallow decision tree to pseudo-residuals; split rules and tree structure are defined in the decision trees topic—here we focus on the sequential loop that trains them.

items.title: Loss functions define the correction target
items.description: The negative gradient of the chosen loss (MSE, log loss, Huber) becomes the target for the next tree; squared error makes pseudo-residuals literal prediction gaps.

items.title: Learning rate as shrinkage per tree
items.description: Each tree is added as F ← F + η·h_m; η controls step size and interacts with n_estimators—too large overshoots, too small needs more trees.

items.title: Production GBM libraries
items.description: XGBoost and LightGBM implement the same sequential residual-fitting idea with histogram splits, regularization, and parallelization optimizations for large tabular data.

items.title: Contrast with parallel bagging
items.description: Random forests train decorrelated trees in parallel and average votes; gradient boosting trains trees sequentially to fix remaining errors—different variance reduction strategy.
